[[RunningHead]]

On the Relationship between Affective Mentalization and Depression Severity: Cross-Sectional, Longitudinal, and Experimental Evidence

Klaus Michael Reininger , Bernd Löwe , Timo Hennig , Anne-Kristin Toussaint , Angelika Weigel , Kerstin Maehder , Peer Briken , Steffen Moritz and Hannah Marie Biel

Hannah Marie Biel

Martinistr. 52, Hamburg D-20246, Germany
m.reininger@uke.uni-hamburg.de

Hamburg, Germany

Potsdam, Germany

Hamburg, Germany

Hamburg, Germany
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8601-0143

Hannah Marie Biel  

depression, depressive states, affective mentalization

Background: Low levels of affective mentalization have been linked to depression severity. Objective: Our objective was to examine how affective mentalization relates to and predicts depression severity and whether an affective-mentalization-intervention reduces state-depression severity. Methods: In Study 1, we investigated the cross-sectional association between affective mentalization measured with the Emotion Beliefs Questionnaire and depression severity gauged with the Patient-Health-Questionnaire-9 in a clinical sample of psychosomatic outpatients (N = 729). In Study 2, we examined the lagged longitudinal association in a prospective general-population-MTurk-sample (N = 318). In Study 3, we experimentally tested the effect of an affective-mentalization-intervention on an induced depression state in MTurk-participants (N = 691). Results: Affective mentalization is negatively related to depression severity in patients diagnosed with, rs(668) = -.30, p < .001 (Study 1) and predicted depression severity over time and above the lagged-criterion, β = -0.06, p = .049 (Study 2). Exposing state-depressed participants to an affective-mentalization-intervention significantly reduced depression states (d = 0.38) compared to state-depressed participants and did not differ from no-treatment-control-participants (Study 3). Discussion: Affective mentalization relates to the development and maintenance of depression states and severity, and interventions employing affective mentalization might reduce depression severity.

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

Issue 2 begins with a paper by Rachel Gibbons which, since its publication online on 16 May 2025, has already become the most read article ever published in this journal. Entitled ‘The “Impossibility” of working in the current NHS: sacrifice to a primitive god’, the paper explores the challenges facing the NHS, with a focus on employee experience.

Maddocks (2024) describes how, on any comparison with other nations, the NHS is neither the highest spender nor the lowest performer. For example, the UK spends less per capita than the G7 average or the OECD average yet is ranked third overall in a comparison of 10 of the world’s richest countries (Blumenthal et al., 2024). Despite this, the calls for greater NHS efficiency due to funding constraints seem to have been an ever-present feature of every government since the global financial crisis, 2008. My impression as a clinician working within the NHS since 2006 is that this over-performance relative to funding can be explained by the contributions made by its staff. With teams rarely at their full complement of staff due to huge challenges in recruitment and retention, staff members consistently go above and beyond in order to meet the ever increasing demand for services. Like many, I witnessed this at its most extreme during the COVID-19 pandemic. Whilst providing psychological support to nursing and care staff in a general hospital, they told me that it was expected that they would stay on and work a double shift with no notice, and commonplace for them to be separated from their families for weeks at a time so that they could live within close proximity to their work. This was an all too familiar story across the country.

In her paper, Gibbons explores the underlying psychodynamic factors that perpetuate these ‘impossible’ demands faced by NHS staff. She describes how the NHS has been idealised since its inception, and how this can lead to unconscious phantasies of dependency on omnipotent care amongst staff who are experiencing increasing levels of stress. Gibbons describes the social systems of defence that protect staff from anxiety but obstruct progress and can lead to a punitive and dysfunctional work culture. She concludes that it will be essential to confront these unconscious expectations of ‘perfect care’ if the NHS is to escape these destructive dynamics. Only by balancing compassion with realistic expectations of care can the NHS begin to deliver sustainable, effective healthcare.

In the Deeper Recesses of a Case of Narcissistic Violence, Could One Listen for a Missing Symbiotic Link to Another for a Subject to Come to Be?: Case of J

Priya Tiwari

Priya Tiwari

New Delhi, India
Email: tiwpriya@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1070-6624

Priya Tiwari  

narcissistic violence, juvenile offender, shame, environment mother, intercorporeality

The paper follows a psychotherapy journey with J., an adolescent who perpetrated an irreversible act of violence on a peer. The paper charts through shifts in the therapeutic field with J., a transformation from this state into an emerging sense of a Subject in J. There was a moment of humiliation provoking the act of violence, which might be seen as an act of narcissistic violence. The focus in the paper is on the factors that may have allowed for transformation in this self experience. Indicating a deeper story of shame that betrays the unconscious need for a symbiotic link with some depositories in life. The paper also makes some links to the role of Justice itself as a depository of this level of dependence that has been noted by many object relations thinkers in noting an ineffable subject of the unconscious, skin as creating an intersensorial floor, in foregrounding ‘soma’ and intercorporeality. The author elaborates on the role and significance of the field of relating at a primitive level of dependence that may need to be supported and tolerated even if ambiguously, at first, to come to be subjectivised into an emerging sense of a Subject that can speak.

[[RunningHead]]

Vulnerability, Development, and Dialectic in Supervision

Hanoch Yerushalmi

Hanoch Yerushalmi

48a, Eder Street, Haifa 3475293, Israel
hyerush1@gmail.com

Hanoch Yerushalmi  

supervision, dialectic, mutual recognition, risking the self, developmental urge

The supervisory process requires continuous dialogue about the meanings of reconstructed therapeutic interactions. Drawing on the Hegelian model, this paper suggests that while recognizing each other as independent and autonomous individuals, the supervisee and the supervisor experience an undermining process that leads to personal changes. Despite the vulnerability and emotional pain involved, the dialogue helps them achieve a higher-order conceptualization of the therapeutic interaction, strengthens their working relationship, and expands their self-consciousness as people and professionals. Acknowledging the existential urge to develop enhances the supervisory dyad’s readiness to engage in a dialectical dialogue characterized by mutual recognition, despite the challenges this poses. In addition, experiencing mutual recognition in supervisory dialogues enhances the participants’ capacity for intersubjectivity, which forms a central personal and professional developmental trajectory.

Psychoanalytic Supervision, by Nancy Mc Williams, New York, The Guilford Press, 2021, 237 pp., £37.99 (hardback), ISBN: 1462547990

Rachel Hodgins

Rachel Hodgins

rachel.hodgins@gmail.com

Rachel Hodgins  

The supervisory relationship can be one of the most formative in our training and professional development, but we’re not always very good at talking about it. In contrast to the relationships between trainees and their patients, which are scrutinised at length and in detail, most of what is, or should be, going on in supervision is not talked about explicitly. In this very readable book, Nancy McWilliams does talk explicitly about all sorts of aspects of supervision, from the perspective of supervisees, supervisors, training courses, analysts, and the impact on patients, as well as giving some very helpful historical perspectives and relevant research findings.

One of the first things that struck me about the book was McWilliams’s conversational and pragmatic tone. For example, the final chapter, Getting the Most Out of Supervision, absolutely acknowledges the real-world context for many trainees who may find themselves navigating services where supervision takes place in the context of a high level of burnout in senior staff, and of ‘frequent implicit messages that the most vital concern … is not the care of each patient but the protection and survival of the agency’ (p. 187). In this case she suggests ‘your job is simply to survive as well as you can and be honest with your clients about the limits of the services they receive … [and to] keep reading’ (p. 187). Interpersonal challenges are discussed as well as organisational and political ones – for instance, McWilliams offers thoughts on how to work with supervisors’ idiosyncrasies, and make the best possible use of a supervisory relationship that does not feel constructive but cannot easily be renegotiated. She also includes a section in this chapter called ‘When the Problem is You’, offering straight-talking advice to make honest use of a good therapist or analyst during training and to use your therapy to discuss your supervisor’s feedback: ‘do this even if it stresses you out financially. There is nothing more important than confronting problems in your own psychology that keep coming up in your clinical work’ (p. 201).

The ‘Impossibility’ of Working in the Current NHS: Sacrifice to a Primitive God

Rachel Gibbons

Rachel Gibbons

UK
Email: drrachelgibbons@icloud.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-6989-8689

Rachel Gibbons  

NHS, workplace trauma, healthcare, burnout

This paper explores the current crisis in the NHS, focusing on the ‘impossible’ demands faced by staff and the underlying psychodynamic factors that perpetuate these challenges. Drawing on the author’s experience as a consultant psychiatrist from 2008 to 2018, the paper examines how idealisation of the NHS has fostered an unconscious dependency on a fantasy of omnipotent care, akin to an idealised love. Rising demand, workforce shortages, and financial pressures have exacerbated these unrealistic expectations, contributing to systemic dysfunction. The paper delves into the social systems of defence that protect staff from anxiety but obstruct progress, contributing to a punitive and dysfunctional work culture. It argues that confronting the unconscious expectations of ‘perfect care’ is essential to breaking free from these destructive dynamics. Only by balancing compassion with realistic boundaries can the NHS move toward sustainable, effective healthcare delivery.

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

Earlier this month Dana Amir, a member of this journal’s editorial board, received the Sigourney award. The Sigourney Award Trust, an independent non-profit organization, bestows annual awards in recognition of outstanding work that has advanced psychoanalytic principles. Professor Amir has received this award for her groundbreaking studies of the ways that trauma is conveyed through language. Her research into aspects of language such as word choice, tone, rhythm, and inflection used by perpetrators and victims has led her to encourage both clinicians and patients to focus more on the narrator than the narrative, to reveal the unique way that each story is structured. Amir has identified new routes for therapeutic intervention by mapping the interplay between dissociative and associative forces. She demonstrates that language can be both a symptom of trauma and a vehicle for healing. An example of this work was included in our Special Issue, ‘Time to (re-)integrate dissociation into psychoanalytic psychotherapy’. Her insights into the language of revenge and forgiveness have provided a framework for facilitating dialogue between Israelis and Palestinians. In an article contributed to our Special Issue ‘Psychoanalytic Perspectives on the Israel-Gaza conflict’, Amir shared personal experiences to illustrate the application of her theories to this terrible situation. I am very grateful for her contributions to this journal, and offer my congratulations that her work has been recognised with this important award.

We begin this issue with an article by Marloes Maatkamp, Hilda Derksen, Annette Paarlberg, Anja Joosten, and Jordy Rovers who report on a multidisciplinary Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT) program for people with Severe Somatic Symptom Disorder (SSD). SSD is a prevalent condition that significantly impairs quality of life and places a burden on health services. Emerging evidence suggests that psychotherapy, including DIT, can be a beneficial treatment for SSD. This study, conducted in the Netherlands, explored patients’ perceived outcomes of a multidisciplinary DIT program as well as their perspectives on its working mechanisms. The team used semi-structured interviews to collect information from 13 patients who had completed a six-month treatment. Thematic analysis revealed themes about patients’ experiences, treatment outcomes and perceptions of the working mechanisms. postpartum depression, mentalization, mother-infant psychotherapy, videofeedback intervention change, process research

This single-case longitudinal study examined changes in therapist and patient mentalization over five video-feedback sessions with a mother experiencing postpartum depression. Using qualitative and observational coding, the study analyzed the moment-to-moment evolution of mentalization within therapeutic interactions. The intervention assessed both therapist and patient mentalization, identifying three core themes related to the therapeutic process. Video-feedback contributed to increased maternal mentalization, fostering a more accurate perception of the infant’s needs and emotional states. Improvements in maternal mentalization were associated with reduced anxiety and self-doubt, greater sensitivity, and enhanced attachment behaviors. The participant also reported decreased depressive symptoms and greater confidence in interacting with her infant. Findings indicate that video-feedback provides a structured and reflective space for mothers to observe, interpret, and regulate their caregiving behaviors through guided self-observation and therapeutic scaffolding. These results underscore the role of video-feedback in enhancing mentalization within mother-infant psychotherapy, offering a promising intervention for addressing postpartum depression-related caregiving difficulties. While findings highlight its potential to bridge the gap between perceived and actual caregiving behaviors, further research is needed to examine its mechanisms of change, long-term impact, and clinical applicability.

[[RunningHead]]

Narcissistic Personality Disorder Treated with Transference-Focused Psychotherapy in a Psychiatry Residency Program: Case Report

Jonathan Jair Cerda Ramos and Guillermo Medina Vidales

Guillermo Medina Vidales

Monterrey, México
jonathan.cerdara@uanl.edu.mx

Monterrey, México

Guillermo Medina Vidales  

narcissistic personality disorder, transference-focused psychotherapy, object relations, psychiatry training, case report

Pathological narcissism is organized by a pathological grandiose self, a compensatory, defensive structure in which the individual’s experience of self and others is overshadowed by an idealized version of the self (perceived as superior) that replaces a more realistic self-representation that integrates both strengths and vulnerabilities. Narcissistic personality disorder (NPD) is a complex psychological condition that involves a consistent pattern of behaviors lasting over time and occurring in various social contexts, which can lead to significant impairments in social relationships and occupational functioning. A contemporary object relations model approach provides a theoretical framework for understanding the distorted and often contradictory representations of self and others that underlie the fluctuating mental states of those with pathological narcissism. In transference-focused psychotherapy (TFP) for narcissistic pathology, or TFP-N, the strategies, tactics, and techniques of TFP are adapted and extended with specific modifications to address the special clinical challenges posed by patients with this condition. TFP training serves as a useful, integrative, and dimensional model, which is well-suited for residency programs, allowing residents to gain experience in intensive psychodynamic treatment for personality disorders (PDs). This paper presents a case study of a patient with severe NPD treated in a psychiatry residency program using TFP.

Alan Baban  

In her Foreword to this important book, Nancy McWilliams describes the ‘distortions’ that have altered the way commissioners, professionals and vast swathes of the public have viewed Psychoanalysis and its offshoots. What was once regarded as Freud’s new ‘science of the mind’ – with its idealised implication of some unfathomable answer or treasured secret at the heart of human existence – has with time been viewed by some as unethical or outdated. At the same time, clearly delineated psychoanalytic concepts like the unconscious or transference have been re-purposed in other therapies with more scientific-sounding terms like ‘core beliefs’ and ‘schema-work’. In a review quoted in the front of this edition, Gwen Adshead writes about this hall-of-mirrors effect of modern academia as a version of ‘deep fakes’. When they are in use in a clinical team discussion, my own experience is that psychoanalytic ideas have real practical value, even as the institutional or educational pressure is often to make these important ideas un-real.

What is most impressive about the Cambridge Guide to Psychodynamic Psychotherapy, then, is the even-handed nature of its explorations. It gives – for a start – a lucid and lived-in introduction to the basic theoretical concepts that underpin the psychodynamic approach. The lead authors write in a clear and warm style, avoiding jargon, and evincing a curiosity and open-mindedness that is a real strength of the book. There is an emphasis on theoretical plurality in these opening chapters, and the ‘different slants’ of dynamic approaches – as Adam Polnay and Rhiannon Pugh so neatly put it in their ‘Outline of Psychodynamic Psychotherapy’ – are taken as if they were different ways to slice the same ‘therapy cake’, rather than mutually exclusive or clinically incompatible methods to understand the same therapy encounter. Different patients and life situations can make us recall or lean on different aspects of our training or experience, rather like a passage of music where one section of the orchestra suddenly builds in volume – but the rest of the instruments are all still there, silently present or quietly thrumming along. What matters most, perhaps, is the therapist’s ability to cultivate an internal space where different resonances and frequencies can be duly attended to, as the clinical encounter naturally unfolds. [[RunningHead]]

Understanding How Group-Based Multidisciplinary Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy Works for Severe Somatic Symptom Disorder: A Qualitative Study of Patient Experiences

M. Maatkamp , H. J. Derksen , A. J. C. Paarlberg , J. P. A. Joosten and J. J. E. Rovers

J. J. E. Rovers

Nijmegen, The Netherlands

Venray, The Netherlands

Nijmegen, The Netherlands

Nijmegen, The Netherlands

Weg door Jonkerbos 100, Nijmegen 6534, The Netherlands
j.rovers@cwz.nl
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4636-9412

J. J. E. Rovers  

Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT), Somatic Symptom Disorder (SSD), multidisciplinary therapy, psychodynamic psychotherapy, patient experiences

Severe Somatic Symptom Disorder (SSD) are prevalent and burdensome. Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT) has shown promise for treating SSD, but its mechanisms remain unclear. This study explored both patients’ perceived outcomes of a multidisciplinary DIT program and their perspectives on its working mechanisms by semi-structured interviews with 13 patients who completed a six-month multidisciplinary DIT program. Thematic analysis was used to examine these experiences and perceived working mechanisms. Participants reported improvements in physical symptoms, mental health and daily and interpersonal functioning. Furthermore, five perceived working mechanisms were identified: (1) Facilitating conditions and environment – the importance of a balanced group composition and a healing environment; (2) Synergy of the multidisciplinary approach – how different therapeutic components reinforced each other; (3) The group works – benefits of group cohesion, altruism, and interpersonal learning; (4) Exploring patterns – recognizing and changing maladaptive interpersonal behaviour; (5) Being seen and heard – the centrality of feeling recognized and understood in fostering epistemic trust. These patients’ accounts suggest that the perceived benefits of this program may stem from its capacity to foster epistemic trust, enhance mentalization, and address maladaptive interpersonal patterns. The multidisciplinary approach enhances these therapeutic effects. However, further research is needed to gain deeper insights into potential working mechanisms.

A Written Supervision Agreement for Psychoanalytic Supervision?

C. Edward Watkins , Michael V. Ellis , Rima Viliūnienė , Ioana-Eva Cădariu , Loredana-Ileana Vîşcu and Jennifer L. Callahan

Jennifer L. Callahan

1155 Union Circle #311280, Denton, TX 76203-1280
USA
Clifton.Watkins@unt.edu

Reșița, Romania

Albany, NY, USA

Vilnius, Lithuania

Timișoara, Romania

Richardson, TX, USA

Jennifer L. Callahan  

supervision, psychoanalytic, psychodynamic, agreement, contract

The supervision agreement (also referred to as a contract), while long mentioned as an important part of psychoanalytic supervision, has yet to be addressed in any substantive or concrete way in the psychoanalytic supervision literature. What exactly is a psychoanalytic supervision agreement? Why does it matter? What are its components? How do you implement such an agreement? We consider these questions subsequently, our purpose being to accentuate the written (as opposed to verbal) psychoanalytic supervision agreement as central to (a) making increasingly probable a good supervision beginning and (b) providing a facilitative framework and anchoring container within which the work of supervision can be enabled. We propose that the written supervision agreement, while conceivably valuable at any stage of supervisee development, can be of greatest value to those beginning to provide psychoanalytic treatment services and receive psychoanalytic supervision (i.e. Novice phase supervisees).

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

I am delighted to announce the appointment of Ms Ana Flavia Badue to the role of Assistant Editor. Ana is a Cultural Anthropologist lecturing at two colleges of City University New York. She is undertaking her psychoanalytic training at the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, NYC. She also brings previous publishing experience as both an assistant and guest editor, making her ideally qualified for this role.

We begin this issue with an article by Jeremy Clarke entitled ‘Code Gender. Are the British Psychoanalytic Council’s new professional standards trans-friendly? Yes, if we remember Money-Kyrle’s “facts of life” include a 4th factor’. The paper addresses the complex topic of working psychoanalytically with gender identity issues in the context of the BPC’s new ‘Standards of Conduct, Practice and Ethics’ and the proposed bill to ban conversion practice that has been slowly moving towards legislation since 2018. Clarke draws on his experience as Deputy Chair, Memorandum of Understanding (2020–2022) when he was asked to steer its response to the government consultation on a proposed ban. This provides invaluable context to the development of the BPC’s ‘Standard 5’.

But the paper does not only explore issues of professional conduct and legislation. Taking Money-Kyrle’s ‘Facts of Life’ as the basis for his discussion, Clarke describes how the heteronormative assumptions of psychoanalysis have the potential to be adopted for conversion practises. He proceeds to identify how this can be avoided in clinical practice. Firstly, through a discussion of John Steiner’s description of how the psychoanalytic treatment model has changed over time. Steiner’s fourth model is based on Money-Kyrle’s ‘Facts of Life’ but with realist revisions away from heteronormative assumptions. Secondly, Clarke describes Alessandra Lemma’s ‘Fourth Fact of Life’, the ‘inescapability of embodiment’. He sees this as complementary to Steiner’s fourth model, in which projective identification can be used to help the patient regain lost parts of the self. Importantly, Lemma explicitly eschews heteronormativity as a predetermined aim in this endeavour. Clarke goes on to propose his fourth factor, ‘epistemic justice’, which can provide us with a helpful capacity to acknowledge our in-built tendency to heteronormativity. The paper concludes with three clinical vignettes illustrating epistemic injustice in psychoanalytic work with ‘other’ genders throughout history, and how Clarke’s fourth factor of epistemic justice can be applied.

Code Gender. Are the British Psychoanalytic Council’s New professional Standards Trans-Friendly? Yes, if We Remember Money-Kyrle’s ‘Facts of Life’ Include a 4th Factor

Jeremy Clarke

Jeremy Clarke

London, UK
Email: therapy@just-therapy-practice.com

Jeremy Clarke  

gender wars, conversion practices and perverse ‘cures’, epistemic justice as the 4th factor; Code Gender

Standard 5 in the new British Psychoanalytic Council code of practice explicitly prohibits conversion practice: ‘intentionally seeking to change someone’s sexual orientation or gender identity’. Whilst the former is no longer at issue, there is fierce disagreement about the latter, and British ‘exploratory’ analysts stand accused of being transphobic. This paper describes the background to Standard 5 and prospect of a trans-inclusive legislative ban in Parliament. The jeopardy for psychoanalysis is that without consensus our clinical ethics can be hijacked. By revisiting Money-Kyrle’s ‘facts of life’, adding a 4th factor into the mix of modern identity, we will better protect our discipline from perverted misuse by conversion therapists.

Sexual Fantasies as Unique Pathway to Understanding Psychodynamics – Do Our Theories Pass the Empirical Test?

Lynn Helen Christien Gaertner , Klaus Michael Reininger and Peer Briken

Peer Briken

Hamburg, Germany
Email: l.gaertner@uke.uni-hamburg.de
http://orcid.org/0009-0001-1734-7698

Hamburg, Germany
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-6671-0465

Hamburg, Germany

Hamburg, Germany
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1360-014X

Peer Briken  

psychodynamic, intrapsychic conflicts, sexual fantasies, individual excitability, Operationalized Psychodynamic Diagnostics

Psychoanalytical theories have always linked intrapsychic conflict to psychosexuality. One’s sexual fantasy is commonly understood as non-consciously representing past (un-)pleasurable experiences, object relations, and intrapsychic conflict, granting unique access to an individual’s psychodynamic. We subjected this idea to empirical testing by assessing intrapsychic conflicts and sexual fantasies in an anonymous online-survey (N = 401). Structural-equation-modeling was used to analyze the data. Respondents using more progressive coping strategies, e.g. for dependency or self-worth conflict, showed more sexual fantasies with externally directed (β = .26; β = .42) and self-directed aggression (β = .23; β = .22). While externally directed aggression is typical in progressively coping individuals, self-directed aggression typically is repressed. Regressive coping, e.g. with self-worth or guilt conflict, was predominantly linked to sexual fantasies with externally directed aggression (β = .38; β = .41), which is typically repressed in regressively coping individuals. These findings underline the clinical value of exploring sexual fantasies as they might help establish access to repressed aggressive strivings. Furthermore, conflicts emerging later in development, such as self-worth and oedipal conflicts, showed stronger associations with aggression-related sexual fantasies, suggesting that better personality functioning might facilitate integrating aggressive and libidinal impulses, and regulating them intrapsychically within sexual fantasies. Our findings provide initial evidence supporting psychoanalytic theory and encourage further interdisciplinary research.

Examining the phenomenon of resistance in psychotherapy both promotes a profound understanding of mental health disorders and supports the psychotherapeutic treatment of patients. However, in-depth, targeted research on resistance is limited. The present review provides an overview of recent empirical research on resistance in psychotherapy. A systematic search on PubMed and PsycInfo for peer-reviewed studies on the phenomenon of resistance in psychotherapy published in the years 2000–2022 in English or German language was conducted. Twenty-two publications fulfilling the search criteria were found. Fifteen out of 19 clinical studies (79%) approach resistance using the concept of cognitive-behavioral therapy. Four out of 19 clinical studies (21%) investigate resistance in psychoanalytic or psychodynamic psychotherapy. The studies identified indicate a strong relationship between patient resistance and psychotherapeutic outcome. Furthermore, resistance appears to be related to aspects of the therapeutic relationship, such as therapist behavior, empathy of therapist, alliance and attachment. Empirical research on resistance in psychotherapy is an interesting, promising field of research. The present study shows a profound gap in empirical research on resistance in psychoanalytic and psychodynamic psychotherapy. A reliable and valid instrument to measure resistance based on psychoanalytic and psychodynamic definitions of resistance is needed.

The negative aspects of trauma, fearful attachment, CPTSD, symptomatic expression, sexual violence

This study illustrates the complex relationships between the negative effects of trauma, attachment patterns, and the symptomatic expressions associated with trauma-related diagnoses (PTSD and CPTSD). The patient’s psychological processes were explored through a clinical semi-structured interview (Adult Attachment Interview) and self-administered questionnaires, including the Relationship Scales Questionnaire (RSQ), the Parental Bonding Instrument (PBI), and the PTSD Checklist Scale (PCLS). Although the patient does not meet diagnostic criteria for PTSD or CPTSD, she exhibits signs related to the negative aspect of trauma as well as a fearful attachment pattern. The expression of the negative aspect of trauma shows some overlap with the fearful attachment pattern and certain core symptoms of CPTSD, while retaining distinct clinical features. This case study offers a detailed examination of how attachment and trauma-informed assessment can deepen our understanding of psychological trauma in a woman with migratory backgrounds who experienced sexual violence. Convergence between negative aspect of trauma, fearful attachment patterns, and core symptoms of CPTSD suggests not merely a correlation, but a deeper structural connection between attachment disturbances and complex trauma. Future research should investigate how integrating the negative aspects of trauma within attachment-informed and psychodynamic frameworks can enhance therapeutic engagement and outcomes in trauma-affected populations.

David O’Driscoll  

As a social worker in the 1980s, I had to visit a patient with an intellectual disability in one of the long stay hospitals that was still functioning at the time. I was just leaving when another hospital patient approached me, asking if he could get a lift to the bookies. I was unsure about the protocol here, so I approached a nurse who told me that it was fine to give him a lift. During the car journey, he explained an aspect of betting I did not fully understand, and I found myself wondering if he really had an intellectual disability. On my next visit, I bumped into the nurse who told me that he was ‘chronically institutionalised as a result of his twenty-plus years living here’ and described him as having an ‘environmental disability’. Sadly, over the course of my 25 years of NHS employment, I have heard my health colleagues tell many similar stories. So how is it that people have been institutionalised like this, and often for the most dubious of reasons such as pregnancy, petty thieving, and vandalism?

Answering this question is at the core of Sarah Wise’s book, which looks at the operation of the most significant legislation for people with intellectual disability, the 1913 Mental Deficiency Act, finally repealed 46 years later in 1959. Once admitted to hospital under this Act there was no right of appeal, with 30% of those admitted being detained for 10 to 20 years and 5% for 30 years. The Act was interpreted in such a way that many people without an intellectual disability, like the patient who wanted a lift in my car, were institutionalised for significant periods of time. ‘Thousands of lives were wasted in the mental deficiency system in the forty-six years of the Act’s operation – a colossal squandering of human potential’ (p. 236).

Wise is an award-winning scholar, specialising in the Victorian period, who has delved into the history of the Mental Deficiency Act, the debacles around it and its impact today. She is particularly interested in the category of ‘moral imbecile’, one of four categories in the 1913 Act. Three categories roughly correspond to the categories of intellectual disability today, but ‘moral imbecile’ is different, based as it is on dubious claims about a person’s behaviour. It was used to describe individuals who were believed to have some permanent mental defect coupled with strong vicious or criminal propensities on which punishment had little or no effect. It was used as catch-all category, often applied to sexually active young women or young men who were deemed to be petty criminals. David Sugar  

Don Carveth is a training analyst with the Toronto Psychoanalytic Society and a professor of sociology at York University in Canada. I first came across him through his YouTube videos, where he shares lectures and discussions on the history of psychoanalytic thinkers as well as his take on more contemporary discussions. It is unusual for an analyst to be so active online and to share so many resources, and much of what he shares feels accessible to the interested.

This book is a short and pithy pulling together of his ongoing areas of interest in psychoanalysis and where they intersect with the social. Namely, his re-evaluation of conscience and morality, and a return to the clinical importance of superego issues and unconscious guilt and their broad impacts upon our social world. At its core, this book has a story, and it goes like this: Freud made a mistake in his structural model when he included conscience in the superego. Rather, conscience should be understood as its own unique structure, capable of internal conflict with other structures. Carveth seeks to untangle the theoretical and conceptual difficulty he believes that we have inherited from Freud’s structural model, and in doing so, he tells us that he is dusting off a little-known book by Eli Sagan titled Freud, Women, and Morality: The Psychology of Good and Evil (1988), a text which he sees as having been overlooked as the first to differentiate the superego and conscience as different psychic structures. Carveth suggests that Totem and Taboo, Freud’s founding myth of the primal father and the introduction of the moral injunction, contains a contradiction: that the superego emerges both as the cause and effect of guilt. Klein attempts to rectify this by bypassing the need for the phylogenetic story and instead turning toward the infant’s experience of the breast, with all the complexity of building towards whole object relations and reparative concern.

Carveth argues that many theorists describe the development of the capacity to think as the way that the superego can be replaced or kept in check. He implicitly puts forward the idea that the loving concern of the other is also internalised: that we are not just dependent upon the building blocks of thought, which he characterises as a kind of cold rationalism. This emphasis on thinking he places at the feet of Bion and contemporary Kleinians, who he claims have returned to more positivist aims in the capacity of the ego. It’s an interesting claim, and not one I’ve heard before. Paul Cundy

London, UK

Paul Cundy  

Supervision is an aspect of practice that dates back to the origins of our discipline. In our opening article, Edward Watkins, Michael Ellis, Rima Viliūnienė, Ioana-Eva Cădariu, Loredana-Ileana Vîşcu and Jennifer Callahan explore how the process is agreed between supervisee and supervisor. ‘A written supervision agreement for psychoanalytic supervision?’ gives an overview of the history of the contract or agreement in supervision, and ponders the question of why written, concrete agreements are rarely encountered in psychoanalytic practice. The paper describes the contributions of Max Eitinger, and the important work of Fleming and Benedek in conceptualising the ‘learning alliance’. The authors state their preference for a written, rather than verbal, agreement. They consider the components of such an agreement, its implementation and relevance. They aim to highlight the function of a written agreement in effecting a satisfactory beginning to the supervisory relationship and providing a facilitative framework and anchoring container within which the work can proceed. They describe four components of the agreement: professional disclosure and supervisory description; the supervisee’s rights; the supervisee’s responsibilities; and typical supervision experiences. The authors recognise that a written agreement is not a panacea, but suggest that it can be a potentially helpful tool. In their view, the written supervision agreement has been overlooked in psychoanalytic practice leading them to advocate for it and consider how its use can benefit the psychoanalytic supervision process. They highlight its particular benefits for supervisees in the early stages of their therapist development process, and hope that it might be of benefit to psychoanalytic supervisors as well.

Like supervision, the ‘case study’ is as old as psychoanalysis itself. We currently find ourselves challenged as psychoanalytic practitioners to meet the twin demands of providing clinical exemplifications (data) whilst at the same time protecting patients’ rights to confidentiality. In our next paper, entitled ‘Responsible creativity: combining clinical report and literary writing in the psychodynamic case study’ Aner Govrin offers a theoretical conceptualisation of the case study. Whilst bearing some resemblance to other forms of creative non-fiction such as memoirs, historic novels and biographies, he argues that the psychodynamic case study combines medical report and literary elements to create a unique and sophisticated hybrid genre. None of these genres claim to represent an objective reality. Instead, psychoanalytic writers use their imagination and literary skills to elicit an identification with the therapeutic process from their readers. [[RunningHead]]

Responsible Creativity: Combining Clinical Report and Literary Writing in the Psychodynamic Case Study

Aner Govrin

Aner Govrin

Ramat Gan
Aner Govrin, Mapu 28, Tel Aviv 643426, Israel
govrina.biu@gmail.com

Tel-Aviv, Israel

Aner Govrin  

Psychodynamic case study, literary genre, psychoanalytic writing, clinical report, psychodynamic formulation

Though the case study is deeply embedded in psychoanalytic history, it has, so far, gone without theoretical conceptualization and has not been considered as a genre in its own right. I argue that the psychodynamic case study combines medical report and literary writing elements to create a unique and sophisticated hybrid genre. It resembles other types of creative non-fiction, like memoirs, historical novels, or biographies. In all these genres, the described reality does not claim to be identical to reality itself. Although analysts do not make things up, they use their imagination and writing skills to elicit their readers’ identification with the therapeutic process. The storytelling allows richer learning by capturing an essence and a deep structure, leaving an impression more lasting than a dry report. However, clinicians don’t have the creative freedom of writers of memoirs and biographies. Writing an exciting story is secondary to helping patients and broadening the community’s knowledge. The blurring of boundaries between facts and creativity presents some pertinent questions: How much creative freedom can writers of case studies take? In the case of forgotten or unknowable details – is it permissible to reproduce them? The article introduces the term ‘responsible creativity’ to suggest some lines that may guide therapists in writing case studies.

[[RunningHead]]

Unbalancing the Imbalance: A Qualitative Exploration of Why Patients Drop Out of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Payvand Jalali , Fariba Zarani , Leili Panaghi and R. Dennis Shelby

R. Dennis Shelby

Daneshjoo Blvd., Velenjak, Tehran 1983969411, Iran
f_zarani@sbu.ac.ir

Tehran, Iran

Tehran, Iran

Chicago, IL, USA

R. Dennis Shelby  

treatment dropout, unilateral termination, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, psychodynamic psychotherapy, grounded theory method

Similar to other therapeutic methods, the issue of patient dropout from psychoanalytic psychotherapy presents significant challenges for patients, therapists, and the overall mental health system. While quantitative studies in solution-focused therapies have often explored this issue, a more in-depth understanding can be gained by approaching it qualitatively within the framework of long-term, open-ended psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Eleven patients and four therapists with direct experience of dropout participated in twenty-six in-depth interviews, guided by Corbin and Strauss’s grounded theory methodology. The analysis identified the central theme of dropout as a ‘departure from the imbalanced binary system’, which includes contextual, action-interaction, and consequence categories. The conceptual model underscores key factors contributing to dropout, encompassing both cross-theoretical elements such as the therapeutic alliance and coping strategies, and more specific factors like perceptions of psychoanalytic therapy’s legitimacy in a specific culture and patients’ beliefs about its effectiveness as a therapeutic approach. These findings provide valuable insights into the complex nature of treatment dropout, offering important clinical and scientific implications.

responseSet.title: The Body in Hypochondria: A Lacanian Perspective
responseSet.authorMast: Burcu Pınar Bulut and  Özlem Bozo
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Body in Hypochondria: A Lacanian Perspective

Burcu Pınar Bulut and Özlem Bozo

Özlem Bozo

Ankara 06560, Turkey
burcupinarbulut@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8168-0068

Çankaya Ankara, Turkey
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-4480-1876

Özlem Bozo  

Hypochondria, body, anxiety, Lacan, Freud

‘Body’ has an essential role in understanding hypochondria in the psychoanalytic approach. As in Freud’s point of view, Lacan argued that the subject’s relation to his/her body is not a pre-given; in the beginning, there is no psychic representation of the body. The medical approach evaluates the symptomatic body as the biological body; nevertheless, it is the body incorporating the language order. Hence, hypochondriac symptoms in a neurotic individual are not based on the biology itself but his/her own ‘understanding’ of the biological anatomy. In this essay, it is posited that there is a dialectic relationship between mind and body by describing Lacan’s ideas on the body in hypochondria, a condition currently referred to as health anxiety. Lacan’s perspective on the body enables us to rethink hypochondria more dynamically and interactively by moving beyond binary oppositions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion: An Introduction, by Nicola Abel-Hirsch, Bicester Oxfordshire, Phoenix Publishing House, 2023, 152 pp., £16.19 (pback), ISBN: 978-1912691838

David Sugar

David Sugar

davidsugar@gmail.com

David Sugar  

Writing an introductory text on Bion is no easy task, though Nicola Abel-Hirsch is well placed to take up the challenge. A training analyst at the British Society, she talks and publishes widely on Bion having previously authored ‘Bion: 365 Quotes’ (Abel-Hirsch, 2019). While her new book, ‘Bion: An Introduction’, is designed for new readers, there is plenty of value to those experienced in Bion’s work, featuring concise summaries of his papers, insights from contemporary thinkers, and engaging anecdotes and commentaries. Among the many introductions to Bion, such as those by Leon Grinberg, Donald Meltzer, Gérard Bléandonu, and Neville and Joan Symington, Abel-Hirsch’s book stands out for its compactness and directness, making it a good entry point. The book reasserts Bion’s Kleinian foundations and it places his concept of container/contained at the center of his theoretical development. By taking a biographical approach, Abel-Hirsch traces the evolution of Bion’s thought, structuring the book chronologically to weave together his external biography, writings, and some conjecture about how his ideas developed over time.

Abel-Hirsch works to keep a threaded narrative throughout the book, going backwards and forwards to help illustrate biographically where Bion might have been in his intellectual journey. A particular attention is given to the traumatic impact of his war time experience which Abel-Hirsch uses to explain his theoretical and emotional journey. This works well, as it grounds him, adding a relatable narrative inviting readers in who might otherwise be discouraged by his more abstract writings. It also helps to avoid the idealisation often attached to writings on Bion.

With this reading you get a sense of Bion grappling with what it means to hold onto something in the face of great strain, whether it be in the intensity of the consulting room or in the face of war time conflict. It made me think of his attempts to maintain links inside himself, the difficulties in maintaining connections in the face of the emotional bombardments of his own life. It also reminds us of his important contributions to contemporary trauma theories and our work in helping more actively dysregulated patients.

Understanding the Psychodynamics of Bereavement Anniversary Reactions: A Systematic Review of the Clinical Case Literature

Rachel Gibbons , Edouard Leaune , Pauline Lau-Tai and Alexandra Pitman

Alexandra Pitman

London, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0901-3038

Lyon, France

Lyon, France

Bron, France

London, UK
a.pitman@ucl.ac.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9742-1359

Alexandra Pitman  

bereavement, grief, mourning, anniversary, psychoanalysis, psychodynamics

Bereavement anniversary reactions, psychological, somatic, or behavioural responses triggered by dates associated with a significant loss, are frequently encountered in clinical practice but remain underexplored within psychodynamic frameworks. This systematic review aimed to identify and summarise published case studies describing anniversary reactions to bereavement and to assess whether these reports provide evidence relevant to psychoanalytic theory. A comprehensive search of five databases yielded 12 case studies reporting on 18 individual patients. These were reviewed through a descriptive and then psychoanalytic lens to identify patterns in symptoms, timing, type of loss, and contextual factors. Most involved sudden or traumatic deaths with mixed psychological and physical symptoms, often delayed or recurrent. A high proportion of cases showed anniversary reactions emerging long after the loss or recurring across multiple years. Our psychodynamic analysis of the material suggested an alignment of these anniversary reactions with established psychoanalytic concepts including, unconscious identification, in which aspects of the deceased are taken into the self and re-enacted at symbolic temporal markers, and the repetition compulsion, the drive to unconsciously re-live unresolved loss or trauma through symptom recurrence rather than symbolic mourning. This review highlights the clinical value of recognising anniversary reactions and suggests that deeper understanding of their psychodynamic underpinnings could enhance care for the bereaved.
